coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots embedded silica molecular imprinted polymer as a novel and sensitive fluorescent nanoprobe for reproducible enantioselective quantification of naproxen enantiomers
coredata.dc:creator: Dehghani, Zahra
Akhond, Morteza
Absalan, Ghodratollah
coredata.dc:description: The nanoparticles of carbon quantum dots embedded silica molecular imprinted polymer (CQDs-SiO2@MIP) were utilized as novel, sensitive, repeatable, and reproducible enantioselective fluorescent nanoprobes for enantioselective quantification of naproxen molecules. The combination of MIP and CQDs was the basis for selectivity enhancement in chiral recognition of naproxen enantiomers due to template selectivity of the synthesized CQDs-SiO2@MIP. Introducing naproxen molecules to the system, selectively induced a considerable quenching of the fluorescence of the CQDs-SiO2@MIP with an efficient distinction between S- and R-enantiomers. The method showed a wide linear working range of 0.5–35.0 µM for S-naproxen and 0.5–30.0 µM for R-naproxen. However, the calibration sensitivity for S-naproxen was estimated at 2.0-fold higher than R-naproxen. Also, the detection limits were calculated to be 0.20 and 0.37 µM for S- and R-naproxen, respectively. The relative standard deviations (RSD %) as an index for repeatability and reproducibility of the developed method were estimated to be 1.4% and 4.0% for intra-day and inter-day assays, respectively.

Recommended procedure for chiral recognition of naproxen enantiomers

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The FT-IR spectra of CQDs-SiO2, CQDs-SiO2@NIP, and CQDs-SiO2@MIP. (B) The TEM image of CQDs. (C) The SEM image of CQDs-SiO2 (inset: isolated particle). (D) The SEM image of CQDs-SiO2@MIP.

(A) The UV–Visible, excitation, and FL spectra of native CQDs. (B) The fluorescence spectra of CQDs-SiO2, CQDs-SiO2@NIP, unwashed CQDs-SiO2@MIP, pure CQDs-SiO2@MIP. (C) The fluorescence spectra of CQDs-SiO2@MIP in the presence and absence of 8.0 µM S-Nap. (D) Ability of CQDs-SiO2@MIP for enantioselective detection of Nap in the presence of 30.0 µM of S-/R-Nap.
The fluorescence spectra at various concentrations for R-naproxen (A) and S-naproxen (B). The calibration curves (C).

Stern-Volmer plots for determination of S-naproxen with CQDs-SiO2@MIP or CQDs-@NIP (A). The calibration curves for different mole fraction of S-naproxen in the mixtures with R-naproxen. Inset: fluorescence spectra for different mole fractions of S-naproxen (B).
Quantification of S-naproxen in commercial naproxen tablets.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots embedded silica molecular imprinted polymer as a novel and sensitive fluorescent nanoprobe for reproducible enantioselective quantification of naproxen enantiomers

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Massoumi Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Shiraz University, Shiraz 71454, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Massoumi Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Shiraz University, Shiraz 71454, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The nanoparticles of carbon quantum dots embedded silica molecular imprinted polymer (CQDs-SiO2@MIP) were utilized as novel, sensitive, repeatable, and reproducible enantioselective fluorescent nanoprobes for enantioselective quantification of naproxen molecules. The combination of MIP and CQDs was the basis for selectivity enhancement in chiral recognition of naproxen enantiomers due to template selectivity of the synthesized CQDs-SiO2@MIP. Introducing naproxen molecules to the system, selectively induced a considerable quenching of the fluorescence of the CQDs-SiO2@MIP with an efficient distinction between S- and R-enantiomers. The method showed a wide linear working range of 0.5–35.0 µM for S-naproxen and 0.5–30.0 µM for R-naproxen. However, the calibration sensitivity for S-naproxen was estimated at 2.0-fold higher than R-naproxen. Also, the detection limits were calculated to be 0.20 and 0.37 µM for S- and R-naproxen, respectively. The relative standard deviations (RSD %) as an index for repeatability and reproducibility of the developed method were estimated to be 1.4% and 4.0% for intra-day and inter-day assays, respectively.

ce:section.ce:para: Chiral compounds, for instance, mandelic acid [1], naproxen [2,3], amino acids [4,5], imatinib [2], nalbuphine [2], gemifloxacin [2], and a wide variety of chemicals with one or more chiral centers have been utilized in pharmaceutical formulations [6]. Regulators approve chiral drugs only in the single enantiomeric form due to different biological activities of these enantiomers in living systems during biomedical processes [6–8]. Hence, a simple, sensitive, and selective determination procedure for each enantiomer is necessary for stability studies of clinical monitoring as well as drug production and especially quality control [6,9–11].

The significance of developing enantioselective analytical sensors and biosensors, as well as designing preparative methods, for enantioseparation purposes is well recognized in biological processes [9]. For this purpose, several techniques including liquid chromatography/mass spectrometry (LC/MS) [11], high performances liquid chromatography (HPLC) [12,13], liquid chromatography/electrospray ionization tandem mass spectroscopy (LC‐ESI‐MS/MS) [14], solid-phase extraction [15], microbalance sensors [10], and colorimetric-based sensors [16–20] have been developed. However, some of these techniques such as HPLC and LC‐ESI‐MS/MS are not simple but expensive and time consuming either [12–14]. Moreover, the sensitivity and selectivity of colorimetric based enantioselective sensors are limited [16–20]. To overcome these challenges, several fluorescence-based sensors have been designed for enantioselective determination of chiral compounds [21–23]. It should be mentioned that the fluorescence-based sensors are attractive for the trace analysis of analytes, especially chiral compounds, due to their simplicity, sensitivity, and good selectivity as well as cost-effective features [24–26]. The performances of these sensors are based on changing fluorescence (quenching or enhancing) of the analytical probe in the presence of an analyte [21–27].

Nanomaterials and especially fluorescent nanomaterials such as carbon dots, quantum dots, and metal-based nanoclusters have been considered as good candidates for designing enantioselective nanosensors and nanobiosensors [28–31]. The attraction of fluorescent nanomaterials for designing optical sensors is due to their unique optical properties, including narrow emission and broad excitation band as well as high quantum yield and good photo-stability [32–34]. Among these nanomaterials, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are the respectable choice because of their biocompatibility and high fluorescent emission [35].

Molecular imprinted polymer (MIP) is a type of biomimetic polymers that is synthesized through polymerization of the cross-linking and functional monomers in the presence of a template molecule [36,37]. Removal of the template molecules from the resulted MIP provides definite cavities with both chemical functionality and shape selectivity of the template molecules. Therefore, MIP technology can be employed as one of the most efficient approaches for improving the selectivity of CQDs to develop efficient nanosensors and fluorescent nanoprobes with advantages of high selectivity of MIP coupled with high sensitivity of fluorescent emission of CQDs [38,39].

It should be mentioned that one of the most attractive capabilities of resulted MIPs is their enantioselective binding properties for chiral templates [40–42]. These chiral MIPs have characteristic advantages such as ease of preparation, scalability, low material costs, and more stability toward thermal and mechanical stress as well as acidity or basicity effects compared with conventional chiral selectors [26,36,37,43].

In this study, a straightforward, simple, sensitive, repeatable, and reproducible enantioselective method was fabricated for recognition of S- and R-naproxen enantiomers using carbon dots embedded silica molecular imprinted polymer (CQDs-SiO2@MIP) as a novel enantioselective fluorescent nanoprobe. The method was constructed based on the quenching of the fluorescence intensity of the fluorescent nanoprobe in the presence of naproxen enantiomers. Introducing naproxen molecules to the system selectively induced a considerable quenching of the fluorescence of the CQDs-SiO2@MIP with an efficient distinction between S- and R-enantiomers. It should be mentioned that the fluorescent nanoprobe was characterized by FT-IR, XRD, EDX, SEM, TEM, and fluorescence spectroscopy. Moreover, the point of zero charge and storestability were checked. Effective parameters on the enantioselectivity of CQDs-SiO2@MIP, including pH, buffer type, and concentration as well as response time were optimized. The calibration plots were constructed for both enantiomers and the performance of CQDs-SiO2@MIP toward sensitive detection of naproxen was verified by comparing with CQDs-SiO2@NIP. Then, the selectivity, foreign species effect on the enantioselective determination of naproxen as well as the quenching mechanism of the fluorescent nanoprobe in the presence of naproxen were investigated. The non-imprinted polymers capped CQDs (abbreviated as CQDs-SiO2@NIP) were also fabricated as a control.

Glucose, maltose, fructose, FeCl2, NaCl, KCl, Zn(NO3)2, Cu(NO3)2, EtOH, (3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTES), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), NaOH, HOAc, tris(hydroxymethyl)-aminomethan, NH4F, and NH3 were from Merck Company. Citalopram, propranolol, ibuprofen, phosphoric acid, naproxen enantiomers from Sigma-Aldrich Company, and deionized water (DW) from Zolal Teb Shimico (Iran) Company were obtained.

A Perkin-Elmer LS50B spectrofluorometer, equipped with a thermostated cell compartment and 1.0-cm quartz cuvettes was used for all fluorometric measurements. A Metrohm model 780 pH-meter was utilized for pH measurements. The FT-IR spectra were recorded by employing an FT-IR spectrometer (spectrumRXI, PerkinElmer) equipped with CaF2 circular cell windows. A transmission electron microscope (Zeiss, model EL10C) operated at an accelerating voltage of 80 kV was used to obtain the TEM images. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was done by TESCAN-Vega 3 from TESCAN Company (Czech Republic). The XRD spectra were recorded by employing Rigaku D/max-3C (Japan, www.rigaku. com/en) with Cu-Kα radiation line (λ = 1.54 nm). The EDX spectra were recorded by employing MIRA3-TESCAN (15 KV, www.tescan.com) electronic microscope.

The CQDs were synthesized via a simple hydrothermal method based on the literature [44]. Briefly, 400.0 mg of glucose powder (as the carbon source) was added to 40.0 mL of deionized water. The resulting homogenous glucose solution was transferred to an autoclave afterward. The synthesis process was completed through the heating of the solution at 180.0 ± 1.0 °C for 6 h. The yellow produced solution was cooled down to ambient temperature and then was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 30.0 min. The final product (CQDs) was collected and stored at 4 ± 1 °C for characterization and further uses.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQDs-SiO2, CQDs-SiO2@MIP, and CQDs-SiO2@NIP
ce:section.ce:para: The MIP was synthesized on silica-coated CQDs (CQDs-SiO2) via modifying an already reported sol–gel polymerization method [28] by utilizing S-naproxen enantiomer as the template. In a typical experiment, 5.0 mL of CQDs (2.7 mg mL−1) was added to 75.0 mL EtOH, followed by the addition of 356.0 µL APTES and the resulting solution was sonicated for 5.0 min. After that, the mixture was heated at 40.0 ± 1.0 °C for 30.0 min under stirring (500 rpm). The mixture was cooled down to the room temperature to collect the produced CQDs-SiO2. To synthesis CQDs-SiO2@MIP, initially, 1.0 mmol S-naproxen as a template was added to 6.5 mg mL−1 CQDs-SiO2 (in 75.0 mL EtOH). The procedure was proceeded through adding 356.0 µL APTES (as the functional monomer) and 1368.0 µL TEOS (as the cross-linker) followed by sonicating for 5.0 min and degassing with nitrogen for 15.0 min. Then, 240.0 µL of the reaction starter, a solution containing 300.0 mg NH4F, 300.0 mg of hydroxyl ammonium chloride (NH3OHCl), 3.0 mL of 25% NH3, and 11.0 mL of deionized water [45], were added and the resulted mixture was stirred for 6.0 min at ambient temperature. The resulted CQDs-SiO2@MIP was dried in the vacuum at 60 °C for 4.0 h. With the same pathway, the non-imprinted polymers capped CQDs (CQDs-SiO2@NIP) were fabricated, without the addition of template molecules, as a control.

It should be noted that the template molecules can bond to the shape-selective site of MIP via hydrogen bond formation [28,46]. In CQDs-SiO2@MIP, the S-Nap molecules strongly bonded to the –NH2 groups of the binding sites of the developed nanoprobe during CQDs-SiO2@MIP synthesis. Hence, the acidified methanol (i.e. 1:10 (v/v) HOAc: methanol) was selected as an efficient solvent for complete removal of template molecules from the binding sites of the nanoprobe. It should be mentioned that the pure methanol cannot completely remove the template molecules from CQDs-SiO2@MIP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recommended procedure for chiral recognition of naproxen enantiomers
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical experiment, 1.0 mL of CQDs-SiO2@MIP stock solution (1.0 mg mL−1) was added to 1.0 mL of 0.05 M acetate buffer (pH, 4.0). After that, the appropriate amount of R- or S-naproxen sample solution was injected into the mixture and thoroughly mixed at ambient temperature for 20.0 min. The fluorescence spectra of CQDs-SiO2@MIP were recorded in the emission wavelengths range of 400.0–600.0 nm (λmax, 445.0 nm) with an excitation wavelength of 350.0 nm. Probing the F0/F parameter was used as an index for optimization procedure where F and F0 refer to the fluorescence intensities in the presence and absence of naproxen enantiomers, respectively. It should be mentioned that the difference between F0/F values of R- and S-enantiomers, i.e. Δ(F0/F), was employed as the analytical response for enantioselective determination of S- and R-naproxen.

The as-synthesized pure CQDs-SiO2@MIP was characterized, as discussed below, by FT-IR, XRD, EDX, SEM, TEM, and fluorescence spectroscopy. Moreover, the point of zero charge and storestability of the CQDs-SiO2@MIP were checked. The FT-IR spectra of CQDs-SiO2, CQDs-SiO2@NIP and CQDs-SiO2@MIP are shown in Fig. 1 A. Considering the FT-IR spectrum of CQDs-SiO2, the broad band at 3413 cm−1 is characteristically attributed to the OH group of CQDs. The peaks at 796.25, 1060, and 3446 cm−1 are related to SiOH, SiOSi bonds, and OH stretching vibrations in SiO2, respectively. These data indicated that the silica was successfully attracted on the surface of CQDs as also reported by other researchers [28,45]. Regarding the FT-IR spectrum of CQDs-SiO2@NIP, the peaks at 1138, 1590, and 3148 cm−1 are assigned to CN [47], NH of primary amines [48,49], and OH [45] vibrations, respectively. In the spectrum of CQDs-SiO2@MIP, the broad peaks around 1630 and 3415 cm−1 can be assigned to –NH bond and the presence of an amide bonding (i.e., -CONH-) in the structure [28,45,50] revealed the copolymerization of APTES and TEOS on the surface of CQDs-SiO2 and formation of CQDs-SiO2@MIP [28].

Moreover, the EDX analysis was performed to prove the successful synthesis of CQDs-SiO2@MIP, CQDs-SiO2, CQDs-SiO2@MIPs, and CQDs-SiO2@NIPs. The EDX pattern of CQDs-SiO2 is shown in Fig. S1A where the presence of the Si peak proved the successful Si-coating of CDs. The EDX spectrum of CQDs-SiO2@MIP is shown in Fig. S1B. The Si characteristic peak and the peaks related to N and C atoms indicated to the successful synthesis of the CQDs-SiO2@MIP. The EDX semi-quantitative results indicated that the weight percentages of C, N, O, and Si atoms in CQDs-SiO2@MIP were 29.67, 10.21, 49.06, and 11.06, respectively. This revealed that the CQDs-SiO2@MIP has a possible empirical formula of C420O522N124Si67. The EDX analysis of CQDs-SiO2@NIP, as shown in Fig. S1C, revealed the presence of Si, N, and C lines. The EDX semi-quantitative results indicated that the weight percentages of C, N, O, and Si atoms were 33.66, 10.87, 45.21, and 10.25, respectively. This indicated that CQDs-SiO2@NIP and CQDs-SiO2@MIP may differ in having shape-selective cavities but not in their compositions.

The XRD patterns of both CQDs-SiO2@MIP and CQDs-SiO2@NIP were recorded for investigation of their crystallinity and estimation of their average size (Fig. S2). As shown in this figures, the peaks in 2θ = 13.186, 21.620, 50.705, 53.585, 59.346, 66.393 in the XRD pattern of the CQDs-SiO2@MIP are related to C(111), C(221), C(444), C(640), C(651), C(662), respectively, indicating to a face-centered cubic structure for the CQDs-SiO2@MIP. Moreover, their crystalline size has been estimated to be about 80 nm. In the case of the XRD pattern of the CQDs-SiO2@NIP, the peaks in 2θ = 13.135, 29.334, 43.290 are related to C(111), C(400), and C(531), respectively, indicating to a face-centered cubic crystalline structure for the CQDs-@NIP. Based on these results, both CQDs-SiO2@MIP and CQDs-SiO2@NIP had the same crystalline structure.

Both TEM and SEM imaging methods were, respectively, used for investigation of the size and morphology of the CQDs, CQDs-SiO2, and CQDs-SiO2@MIP. The TEM image of CQD is shown in Fig. 1B. Regarding this figure, the CQDs had an average size of about 10 nm. The SEM image of CQDs-SiO2 in Fig. 1C indicates to the formation of uniform particles with a size distribution of 40–60 nm and a mean size of 50 nm. As seen in Fig. 1B, C, the CQDs and CQDs-SiO2 had similar spherical morphology showing that the morphology of CQDs was not damaged after silica coating by solvothermal treatments [51]. It should be mentioned that it is extremely difficult to precisely identify the edges of the nanoparticles in Fig. 1C. Hence, a magnification of the isolated CQDs-SiO2 is provided in the inset of Fig. 1C to provide a better view of CQDs-SiO2 nanoparticles surface. Moreover, there was no evidence for core–shell structure after silica coating with APTES which is in good agreement with previously reported papers [52]. However, the increase in the size of CQDs, after silica coating with APTES, indicated that the silica layer was attached on the surface of CQDs during the silica coating which is in good agreement with the results of FT-IR and EDX analyses.

Also, the SEM image of CQDs-SiO2@MIP in Fig. 1D refers to uniform mono-dispersed nanoparticles having a size range of 65–127 nm with an average value of 74 nm in good agreement with the XRD pattern. Comparing to CQDs-SiO2, the larger size of CQDs-SiO2@MIP nanoparticles is expected due to modification with MIP indicating successful synthesis of the CQDs-SiO2@MIP.

It should be mentioned that the photoluminescence properties of native CQDs were investigated by fluorescence spectroscopy and the results are shown in Fig. 2 A. As shown in this figure, CQDs showed a broad absorption band from 200.0 to 400.0 nm with a maximum wavelength at 230.0 nm. Moreover, the excitation band of the as-synthesized carbon dots showed a symmetric spectrum with a maximum wavelength of 350.0 nm. Hence, the fluorescence spectrum of CQDs was recorded upon excitation at 350.0 nm which revealed a fine symmetric emission spectrum with a λmax of 442.0 nm.

Moreover, the fluorescence properties of CQDs-SiO2, CQDs-SiO2@MIP, and CQDs-SiO2@NIP were checked and the results are shown in Fig. 2B. Upon being excited at 350.0 nm, Fig. 2B, the CQDs-SiO2 nanoparticles showed a λmax of about 443.0 nm with an enhanced fluorescence intensity, comparing to the unmodified CQDs, which is favorable for improving the sensitivity of the measurement. Moreover, the interaction between CQDs and MIP (with silica matrix) must be improved through modification of CQDs with a layer of silica as previously reported in the literature [28,49]. Hence, considering both enhancing the sensitivity and improving the interaction between CQDs and MIP, the CQDs-SiO2 nanomaterials rather than unmodified CQDs were selected for the synthesis of CQDs-SiO2@MIP.

It should be mentioned that the reproducibility of CQDs-SiO2 synthesis was evaluated through performing five replicated syntheses followed by measuring the fluorescence properties of the produced nanoparticles. The λmax for all synthesized nanoparticles was found to be 443.0 nm and the relative standard deviation (RSD) among their fluorescence intensities was 4.5% indicating that the reproducibility in synthesis of the nanoparticles was excellent. In the case of CQDs-SiO2@NIP, when the excitation wavelength was fixed at 350.0 nm, CQDs-SiO2@NIP showed an asymmetric emission spectrum with a λmax of 432.0 nm, indicating a blue shift in the fluorescence spectrum of CQDs after silica coating. Besides, CQDs-SiO2@MIP showed an asymmetric emission spectrum with a λmax of 445.0 nm upon excitation at 350.0 nm.

The surface charges of CQDs-SiO2@MIP were evaluated by the determination of the zero point of charge (ZPC). Thepoint of zero charge explains the condition where the electrical charge density on the surface of an adsorbent is zero and therefore, assigns to the adsorption phenomenon.It should be mentioned that ZPC is defined as the pH value at which, for instance, a solid nano-compound submerged in an electrolyte reveals zero net electrical charges on the surface. The surface charge of the adsorbent at pHs lower than ZPC is positive and at pHs higher than ZPC is significantly negative. The determination of ZPC was carried out based on the method reported by Rivera-Utrilla et al. [53]. To do this, 0.15 mg of CQDs-SiO2@MIP was added to a solution of 1.0 × 10−2 M NaCl. Then, the pH was adjusted to a certain value in the range of 2.9–11.3 using 0.10 M NaOH or HCl. The CQDs-SiO2@MIP were brought into contact with the solution for a period of 24 h. Then, the final pH was measured and the ΔpH was plotted against the initial pH. The ZPC value was estimated from the curve at ΔpH = 0.0. The ZPC value for CQDs-SiO2@MIP was found to be at pH 4.5 (Fig. S3).

The storage stability of the as-synthesized CQDs-SiO2@MIP was investigated at 4.0 °C. To do this, fluorescence spectra of CQDs-SiO2@MIP were recorded at different time intervals (Fig. S4). Regarding this figure, CQDs-SiO2@MIP revealed excellent storage stability which was about 88.0% of its initial fluorescence intensity after being stored for 90 days.

The detection system was constructed based on the quenching of the fluorescence intensity of CQDs-SiO2@MIP in the presence of naproxen molecules. The fluorescence spectra of CQDs-SiO2@MIP in the presence and absence of 8.0 µM of S-naproxen are shown in Fig. 2C. In the presence of S-naproxen, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs-SiO2@MIP was significantly decreased indicating the ability of CQDs-SiO2@MIP for the detection of naproxen molecules as a fluorescent nanoprobe. Moreover, the ability of the developed fluorescent nanoprobe for the enantioselective determination of naproxen enantiomers was checked in the presence of 30.0 µM of each enantiomer and the results are shown in Fig. 2D. According to this figure, introducing naproxen molecules to the system selectively induced a considerable quenching of the fluorescence of CQDs-SiO2@MIP with an efficient distinction between S- and R-enantiomers. Based on the above-mentioned considerations, the CQDs-SiO2@MIP was utilized as a novel, sensitive, repeatable, and reproducible enantioselective fluorescent nanoprobe for enantioselective quantification of naproxen molecules. Moreover, the combination of MIP and CQDs was used as a basis for selectivity enhancement in chiral recognition of naproxen enantiomers due to template selectivity of the resulted CQDs-SiO2@MIP.

Effective parameters on the fluorescence intensity and enantioselectivity of CQDs-SiO2@MIP, including pH, buffer type and concentration as well as the response time were optimized. The fluorescence spectra of CQDs-SiO2@MIP were recorded in the emission wavelengths range of 400–600 nm (λmax = 445.0 nm) with an excitation wavelength of 350.0 nm. Probing the F0/F parameter was used as the index for optimization procedure for the determination of 10.0 µM of both S- or R-naproxen.

The effect of pH as one of the most important parameters on the fluorescence intensity and enantioselectivity of CQDs-SiO2@MIP was investigated in the pH range of 3.0–10.0. As shown in Fig. S5A, the quenching of fluorescence intensity in the presence of S-naproxen was characteristically higher than R-naproxen. Fig. S5A exhibited that the F0/F parameter reached its maximum value when pH was fixed at 4.0 which was considered as the optimum value for performing the subsequent investigations.

The effect of the type and concentration of the buffer solution on the fluorescence intensity and enantioselectivity of CQDs-SiO2@MIP was checked. The effect of buffer type (Fig. S5B) was investigated at different buffers including tris(hydroxymethyl)-aminomethan (Tris), phosphate, universal and acetate buffer solutions. The effect of the buffer concentration (Fig. S5C) was tested in the range of 0.01–0.20 M of the selected buffer. Results indicated that the highest value for the F0/F parameter was observed in acetate buffer (0.05 M, pH 4.0) which was then selected for continuing the chiral recognition of S- and R-naproxen enantiomers.

The response time of a probe, as one of the most important properties showing its applicability in real sample analysis, was checked in different periods up to 40.0 min. Experimental results indicated that the F0/F parameter was reached to its maximum value after 20 min and then leveled off (Fig. S5D).

Quenching of the fluorescence intensity of a fluorescent molecule usually occurs through two different pathways, including complex formation and collisional quenching called as dynamic and static quenching mechanism, respectively [54,55]. The quenching mechanism of CQDs-SiO2@MIP with naproxen molecules was investigated through a temperature-controlled method. To do this, fluorescence quenching spectra of CQDs-SiO2@MIP with various concentrations of naproxen molecules were recorded at three different temperatures. The resulted data were analyzed by the Stern-Volmer equation [54,56]:
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K ST
Q

where [Q] and KST are the concentration of the quencher and Stern-Volmer quenching constant, respectively. Based on the literature [54], if the slope of the Stern-Volmer plot increases by increasing temperature, it can be deduced that the quenching mechanism is dynamic; otherwise, the quenching follows a static pathway. Therefore, the Stern-Volmer plots were constructed as shown in Fig. S6, indicating a slight decrease in the slope of Stern-Volmer plots with increasing temperature. Therefore, it can be concluded that the quenching of CQDs-SiO2@MIP with naproxen molecules occurred through a static mechanism.

For both S- and R-naproxen enantiomers, the Stern-Volmer plots were constructed. The results are shown in Fig. 3 . As shown in this figure, the method showed a linear working range of 0.5–35.0 µM for S-naproxen and 0.5–30.0 µM for R- naproxen while calibration sensitivity for S-naproxen was estimated about 2.0-fold higher than that of R-naproxen. Moreover, the linearity of the calibration graph for S-naproxen (R2 = 0.9926) was characteristically higher than that of R-naproxen (R2 = 0.9737) due to functionality, size, and shape selectivity of the CQDs-SiO2@MIP for S-naproxen enantiomers. Besides, the detection limit was calculated based on the following formula (for 10 replicate measurements at the optimal conditions) [57–59]:
LOD = 3
S b
/ m
where m and Sb are the slope of the calibration curve and the standard deviation of the blank, respectively. The detection limits were estimated to be about 0.20 and 0.37 µM for S- and R-naproxen, respectively.

The repeatability and reproducibility of the method were evaluated for 8.0 µM of S-naproxen. The histograms for intra-day and inter-day assays are shown in Fig. S7A and B, respectively. The RSD% was calculated to be about 1.4% and 4.0% for intra-day and inter-day assays, respectively, indicating good reproducibility for the developed method.

Considering the measurement stability proved by repeatability and reproducibility studies, the reusability of the developed fluorescent nanoprobe was also checked. To do this, the CQDs-SiO2@MIP was recycled after each measurement through centrifuging and then washing it with acidified methanol for removing S-naproxen. The recycled CQDs-SiO2@MIP was reused for the measurement of a certain concentration of S-naproxen. The results showed that after five successive recycling/measurements, the RSD among the results obtained for determination of 8.0 µM S-naproxen was 3.5% (Fig. S7C).

It should be mentioned that the analytical figures of merits of the developed method were compared with some reported methods (Table S1). Based on this table, the developed method showed good analytical figures of merits for naproxen enantioselective determination. It must be noted that the developed fluorescent nanoprobe revealed higher reproducibility for enantioselective determination of naproxen molecules when compared to bovine serum albumin protected gold nanoclusters [54], bromophenol blue [55], metal ion-mediated complex imprinted membrane [56], and ultra-high performance liquid chromatography (UHPLC)-based sensors [60]. However, the inter-day reproducibility of the developed method is lower than reported for the sensor based on bovine serum albumin protected gold nanoclusters. It should be mentioned that CQDs-SiO2@MIP-based method showed a comparable detection limit with other methods. Moreover, the linear dynamic range of the developed method covered very lower naproxen concentration than the sensors based on bromophenol blue, metal ion-mediated complex imprinted membrane, and UHPLC. Hence, it can be deduced that the developed method is a good choice for sensitive and reproducible determination of the naproxen enantiomers.

Investigation of the selectivity of the developed sensor was performed by using analogs compounds to the naproxen molecules, including citalopram, propranolol, and ibuprofen. For exploring the similarity between naproxen and its analogs compounds, their chemical structures are provided in Fig. S8. Regarding the selectivity test, Fig. S9A exhibits that the CQDs-SiO2@NIP did not well differentiate between naproxen molecules and its analogs and consequently, the response for both naproxen and its analogs was approximately equal. But, the naproxen was strongly bonded to the active sites of CQDs-SiO2@MIP and; therefore, caused characteristic quenching in the fluorescence of CQDs-SiO2@MIP. However, comparing with naproxen molecule, its analogs weakly bonded to the active site of CQDs-SiO2@MIP; therefore, the response of naproxen was characteristically higher.

Moreover, for investigating the effect of possible coexisting species on the naproxen determination, some metal ions including Fe2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Na+, and K+ as well as several sugars, for instance, glucose, maltose, and fructose were checked. It should be mentioned that the naproxen concentration was fixed at 8.0 µM in all tests. As shown in Fig. S9B, metal ions had no significant interruption on the naproxen response even at concentrations 20-fold higher than the naproxen concentration. Also, the sugars caused no considerable change in the analytical signal of naproxen even at 50-fold higher than the naproxen concentration. Hence, the combination of MIP and CQDs was the basis for selectivity enhancement in chiral recognition of naproxen enantiomers.

Although the developed fluorescent nanoprobe showed high selectivity for S-naproxen determination against co-existing materials and analog compounds, some degrees of fluorescence quenching were also observed in the presence of R-naproxen (Fig. 2C). However, the developed fluorescent nanoprobe can interact with S-naproxen more selective than R-naproxen. The higher selectivity of CQDs-SiO2@MIP toward S-naproxen is probably due to the presence of many specific structure cavities (i.e., chiral cavities) with a fixed three-dimensional conformation [61] which are fit for selective binding of negatively-charged S-naproxen with the stereoselective positively-charged amino groups on the cross-linker (i.e., APTES) matrix via electrostatic (Meisenheimer complex formation [26]) and hydrogen-bonding [61–65]. In the other word, the difference in binding behavior of the enantiomers is related to their spatial characteristics so that more appropriate interaction sites are provided for S- than R-naproxen [63]. Both enantiomers mutually affected their responses (Fig. 4 B) and the analysis of an enantiomeric mixture (e.g., real media) using a calibration plot (Fig. 3C) showed some degrees of false-positive or false-negative errors. To eliminate these errors, the standard addition method was used for analysis of an enantiomeric mixture (e.g., commercial naproxen tablets) to recognize S-naproxen in the presence of R-naproxen. The procedure showed high ability in solving the enantiomer mutual interference as demonstrated in Table 1 .

Verification of the performance of the designed fluorescent nanoprobe was tested by recording of the fluorescent intensities of CQDs-SiO2@MIP and CQDs-SiO2@NIP at optimal experimental conditions and construction of Stern-Volmer plots in the concentration range of 0.0 to 30.0 µM (Fig. 4A). The fluorescence intensities of CQDs-SiO2@MIP and CQDs-SiO2@NIP were quenched by increasing of the naproxen concentration. The quenching of fluorescence intensity of CQDs-SiO2@MIP was remarkably higher than CQDs-SiO2@NIP. Therefore, it was concluded that CQDs-SiO2@MIP obtained imprinted binding sites that were able to rebind the naproxen molecule selectively. It should be mentioned that CQDs-SiO2@NIP did not show significant performance such as that was observed for CQDs-SiO2@MIP against naproxen molecules proving that the imprinted binding sited have been successfully formed in CQDs-SiO2@MIP. Fig. 4A reveals that the calibration sensitivity of CQDs-SiO2@MIP for S-naproxen determination was thrice higher than the calibration sensitivity of CQDs-SiO2@NIP. Moreover, employing CQDs-SiO2@MIP has improved the linearity of the calibration graph as the value of R2 was about 0.993 and 0.96 for CQDs-SiO2@MIP and CQDs-SiO2@NIP, respectively.

The fluorescence spectra of CQDs-SiO2@MIP were recorded in the presence of the mixture of naproxen enantiomers with different mole fractions of S-naproxen. The total concentration of naproxen was fixed at 10.0 µM in all samples. The calibration curve for S-naproxen in the mixture with R-naproxen was plotted and is shown in Fig. 4B. The figure clearly indicated that the enantiomeric composition of naproxen molecules can be verified from the corresponding calibration curves.

For investigating the capability of the nanoprobe for enantioselective determination of naproxen enantiomers in real samples, three commercial tablets were obtained from Pharmachemie, Sobhandarou, and Tehrandarou companies (Tehran, Iran). For analyzing purposes, five tablets were weighed and powdered, then, a quantity of 100.0 mg naproxen from mixed content of the tablets samples was dissolved in deionized water to prepare a 100.0-mL solution which was subsequently sonicated for 40 min. After filtration, 1.0 mL of the resulting mixture was diluted ten times by adding deionized water. Then, naproxen was determined in the tablets by utilizing the standard addition method (Table 1). The method revealed excellent recovery for naproxen determination.

The accuracy of the developed method for the S-naproxen determination was validated by comparing it with the spectrophotometric reference method reported by Keyhanian et al. [55]. As shown in Table 1, the recovery results for determination of S-naproxen by the developed method (101.5–102.2%) were in good agreement with those obtained from the reference method (98.6–105%). Moreover, a known amount of S-naproxen was added into a powdered commercial tablet of S-naproxen followed by the recovery experiment for accuracy checking. The recovery results (101.0–101.4%), as reported in Table 1, indicated that the developed fluorescent nanoprobe exhibited high accuracy for S-naproxen determination in real media.

Generally, the MIPs are high throughput materials for selective separation of chemical compounds. The synthesis of MIP is based on the copolymerization of the functional monomers and a cross-linker in the presence of a template molecule (target) [26,36,37,42]. After removal of the target molecule, the functional groups on the cross-linker matrix are ready to selectively rebind with the target molecule. This means that there are cavities which are complementary in size and shape to selectively fit the target molecule. It is well known that the selectivity of MIP is due to Van der Waals and/or hydrogen bond formation of the target within the interior of the binding cavity [61–63]. In this study, as discussed in Section 3.2, when S-naproxen was introduced into CQDs-SiO2@MIP solution, it efficiently bonded to the chiral cavities of MIP resulting significant quenching in the fluorescence intensity of CQDs-SiO2@MIP. The quenching may be related to the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) from the negatively-charged carboxyl groups of S-naproxen to the favorite positivity-charged amino groups of the cross-linker present inside the shape-selective binding sites (chiral imprinted cavities) of the CQDs-SiO2@MIP [28,43,54,61,64]. It should be noted that the differences in binding behavior of S- and R-naproxen is related to the differences in their spatial characteristics [64]. On the other hand, the functional groups of cross-linker inside the shape-selective cavities of MIP were fully matched with the stereo-position of S-naproxen functional groups (i.e., they were stereoselective) during the synthesis of chiral imprinted cavities. This caused more efficient and selective interactions (hydrogen bond or electrostatic interactions) between MIP and S-naproxen as reported [61,64–66]. In the other word, the diffusion of R- and S-enantiomers into the MIP cavities is significantly stereoselective; hence, the better performance of S-naproxen for binding to MIP is the stereoselective diffusion [67].

The ZPC value of 4.5 shows that at pH 4.0, which was the optimum pH for determination of naproxen, the amino groups of the cross-linker inside the cavities of CQDs-SiO2@MIP are positively charged. This indicates to a possible quenching through formation of an inclusion complex between negatively-charged carboxyl groups of S-naproxen and positively-charged amino groups inside the cavities of CQDs-SiO2@MIP. Moreover, the enhanced enantioselectivity of the nanoprobe toward S-naproxen at acidic conditions (Fig. S5A) is due to higher degree of protonation of amino groups of the cross-linker. Additionally, the static mechanism (Section 3.4) of quenching of fluorescence of CQDs-SiO2@MIP by naproxen also pointed to the formation of a complex between naproxen and CQDs-SiO2@MIP during the quenching process. Based on these, it is deduced that after diffusion of S-naproxen into the shape-selective cavities of CQDs-SiO2@MIP, it binds via various interactions. First, hydrogen bonding of amino groups in the cavities with carbonyl group and/or etheric oxygen of S-naproxen. Second, charge transferring from aromatic ring of naproxen to positive amino groups in the cavities. Third, formation of a Meisenheimer complex between the positively-charged amino groups presented inside the cavities and the negatively-charged carboxyl group of naproxen [61–65]. A significant quenching in fluorescence intensity of CQDs-SiO2@MIP then may occur due to fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between naproxen and CQDs-SiO2@MIP [26]. Based on the above considerations, a possible mechanism for the enantioselective naproxen determination is schematically presented in Scheme S1.

A novel, straightforward, fast, simple, sensitive, repeatable, and reproducible enantioselective fluorescent nanoprobe was fabricated for recognition of S- and R-naproxen enantiomers using carbon dots embedded silica molecular imprinted polymer (CQDs-SiO2@MIP). The principle of the developed system was based on selective quenching of the fluorescence intensity of CQDs-SiO2@MIP by naproxen enantiomers (with efficient distinction between S- and R-naproxen). This was due to the formation of a Meisenheimer complex between positively-charged amino-groups of CQDs-SiO2@MIP and negatively-charged carboxyl groups of S-naproxen inside the shape-selective cavities of CQDs-SiO2@MIP. Moreover, the selectivity considerations revealed that the analogs compounds to the naproxen could weakly bond to the active sites of CQDs-SiO2@MIP comparing with naproxen molecules due to the functionality, size, and shape-selectivity of CQDs-SiO2@MIP. The method showed a wide linear working range of 0.5–35.0 µM for S-naproxen and 0.5–30.0 µM for R- naproxen while calibration sensitivity for S-naproxen was estimated about twice higher than R-naproxen. The detection limit was calculated to be 0.20 and 0.37 µM for S- and R-naproxen, respectively. The relative standard deviations as an index for repeatability and reproducibility of the method were estimated to be 1.4% and 4.0% for intra-day and inter-day assays, respectively. The feasibility of the nanoprobe for the enantioselective determination of naproxen enantiomers in real samples was performed by analyzing the commercial tablets through performing recovery tests.
